goal: Merged offline knowledge pack combining the existing in-app reference library with new practical, source-backed action cards.

notes: Do not remove the original reference guides.
Surface short practical cards first, then broader scenario guides, then references.
Medical-safety cards should use conservative wording and clear red flags.
Some signaling content is included but flagged for source review before final public release.

guides.preferredOption: Get under sturdy furniture if possible, cover your head and neck, and hold on.
guides.fallbackOption: If no sturdy furniture is nearby, move only a few steps to an interior area away from glass and protect your head and neck.
steps: Drop to hands and knees.
Cover head and neck under sturdy shelter if possible.
Hold on until shaking stops.
Stay indoors if already indoors.
If outdoors, move away from buildings and power lines.
If driving, pull over safely and stay in the vehicle.
warnings: Falling glass, shelves, lights, and appliances can cause serious injury.
Aftershocks may follow.
whatNotToDo: Do not run outside while the building is shaking.
Do not use elevators.
redFlags: Serious injury, heavy bleeding, loss of consciousness, or major structural damage require urgent help.
preparednessTips: Practice Drop, Cover, and Hold On.
Keep shoes and a flashlight near your bed.

sourceReferences.whyUseful: Clear public-facing actions by location.

sourceReferences.whyUseful: Plain-language before/during/after instructions.

sourceReferences.title: Earthquake Preparedness and Response
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical preparedness, hazard, and response guidance.

guides.title: Earthquake Safety: During Shaking
guides.summary: Drop, Cover, and Hold On. Stay where you are and protect your head and neck until the shaking stops.
guides.whenToUse: Use during felt earthquake shaking or an official earthquake alert.

guides.preferredOption: Check people for injuries first, then check for fire, gas leaks, unstable debris, and electrical hazards.
steps: Check yourself and nearby people for injuries.
Look and smell for hazards such as gas leaks and fire.
Leave immediately if you suspect gas, fire, or major structural damage.
Move to an open meeting area away from buildings and power lines if needed.
Be ready to Drop, Cover, and Hold On again during aftershocks.
Follow official instructions via radio or phone alerts.
warnings: Damaged buildings and stairwells may fail without warning.
Downed power lines can cause electrocution.
whatNotToDo: Do not use open flames if gas leaks are possible.
Do not re-enter heavily damaged buildings casually.
redFlags: Gas smell, smoke, trapped people, or major structural cracks require urgent professional help.

sourceReferences.title: Stay Safe During an Earthquake
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Clear public-facing actions by location.

sourceReferences.title: Preparing for Earthquakes
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Preparedness planning and household actions.

sourceReferences.title: Earthquake Safety
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Plain-language before/during/after instructions.

sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical preparedness, hazard, and response guidance.

guides.title: Earthquake Safety: Right After Shaking
guides.summary: Check for injuries and hazards, move to a safer open area if needed, and be ready for aftershocks.
guides.whenToUse: Use immediately after earthquake shaking stops.

guides.preferredOption: Evacuate on foot to higher ground or inland as soon as the shaking stops.
steps: Protect yourself during shaking first.
As soon as it is safe, go inland or to higher ground if strong or long shaking occurred.
Avoid beaches, shorelines, and low coastal roads.
Stay away until officials say it is safe.
warnings: Tsunami waves can arrive quickly after coastal earthquakes.
Multiple waves may follow.
whatNotToDo: Do not go to the shore to watch the water.
Do not return after the first wave.

sourceReferences.title: Stay Safe During an Earthquake
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Clear public-facing actions by location.

guides.title: Earthquake Safety: Tsunami Risk After a Quake
guides.summary: If you are near the coast and strong or long shaking occurs, move to higher ground or inland as soon as it is safe.
guides.whenToUse: Use in coastal or tsunami-risk areas after strong or long earthquake shaking.

guides.preferredOption: Combine a family plan, a practiced drill, and a ready-to-grab supply kit.
steps: Secure bookshelves, cabinets, and heavy appliances.
Move heavy or breakable items lower.
Practice Drop, Cover, and Hold On.
Choose meeting points and an out-of-area contact.
Keep basic supplies ready.

sourceReferences.title: Stay Safe During an Earthquake
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Clear public-facing actions by location.

guides.title: Earthquake Preparedness Checklist
guides.summary: Secure heavy items, practice drills, and keep core supplies ready before an earthquake happens.
guides.whenToUse: Use for household, school, or workplace planning before an earthquake.

guides.preferredOption: Evacuate to higher ground immediately when warnings are issued or water is rising around you.
steps: Monitor official alerts.
Leave low-lying areas and head to higher ground.
Avoid flooded roads and turn around if water covers the route.
If trapped indoors, move upward and signal rescuers if needed.
warnings: Floodwater can hide debris, sewage, chemicals, wildlife, and power lines.
Even shallow moving water can knock a person down or sweep a vehicle away.
whatNotToDo: Do not drive around barriers.
Do not walk, swim, or play in floodwater.
redFlags: If someone is trapped, electrically shocked, or becomes ill after floodwater exposure, seek urgent help.

sourceReferences.title: Turn Around Don't Drown®
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Core floodwater safety rule.

sourceReferences.title: Floodwater After a Disaster or Emergency
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Health risks and post-contact hygiene/safety.

sourceReferences.title: Flood Safety and Preparedness
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Before/during/after flood actions.

guides.title: Flood Survival: Get to Higher Ground
guides.summary: Turn Around, Don't Drown. Never drive, walk, or swim through floodwater. Move to higher ground immediately.
guides.whenToUse: Use during flash floods, river overflows, urban flooding, or rapidly rising water.

guides.preferredOption: Avoid floodwater entirely and use protective footwear and gloves if cleanup or unavoidable contact occurs.
steps: Avoid entering floodwater if possible.
If contact occurs, wash exposed skin thoroughly.
Clean and cover cuts quickly.
Discard heavily contaminated food or materials unless official guidance says otherwise.
warnings: Floodwater may contain sewage, fuel, broken glass, and sharp debris.
Water can energize hidden electrical hazards.
whatNotToDo: Do not let children play in floodwater.
Do not handle downed wires or objects touching them.

sourceReferences.title: Turn Around Don't Drown®
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Core floodwater safety rule.

sourceReferences.title: Floodwater After a Disaster or Emergency
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Health risks and post-contact hygiene/safety.

sourceReferences.title: Flood Safety and Preparedness
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Before/during/after flood actions.

guides.title: Floodwater Safety
guides.summary: Treat floodwater as contaminated and dangerous. Avoid contact whenever possible.
guides.whenToUse: Use when floodwater is present around homes, roads, yards, or cleanup areas.

guides.preferredOption: Wait for official clearance, then inspect cautiously using protective gear and clean lighting.
steps: Wait for local authorities to declare the area safe if possible.
Check for structural instability before entering.
Use flashlights instead of open flames.
Avoid turning on electricity or gas unless you know the systems are safe.
warnings: Mold, sewage residue, gas leaks, and unstable floors can remain after water recedes.
whatNotToDo: Do not use candles in a damaged building.
Do not re-energize flooded electrical systems casually.

sourceReferences.title: Turn Around Don't Drown®
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Core floodwater safety rule.

sourceReferences.title: Floodwater After a Disaster or Emergency
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Health risks and post-contact hygiene/safety.

sourceReferences.title: Flood Safety and Preparedness
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Before/during/after flood actions.

guides.title: Returning Home After Flooding
guides.summary: Do not re-enter flooded or damaged homes until officials say it is safe, and watch for structural, electrical, and contamination hazards.
guides.whenToUse: Use after floodwater has receded and you are considering re-entering a building.

guides.preferredOption: Go to a basement, storm cellar, or interior safe room immediately.
guides.fallbackOption: Use an interior room, hallway, closet, or bathroom on the lowest floor and protect yourself under sturdy furniture if available.
steps: Move quickly to the safest shelter available.
Stay away from windows, large rooms, and exterior walls.
Get low and cover your head and neck.
Remain sheltered until the danger has clearly passed.
warnings: Flying debris causes many tornado injuries and deaths.
Mobile homes and weak structures can fail quickly.
whatNotToDo: Do not go under overpasses.
Do not open windows.
Do not use elevators.
redFlags: After the tornado, gas smell, downed lines, serious injuries, or structural collapse require urgent help.

sourceReferences.title: What to do During a Tornado
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Location-specific shelter instructions.

sourceReferences.title: Stay Safe During a Tornado
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Public safety actions and scenario guidance.

sourceReferences.title: Joint Statement on Tornado Safety
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Consensus best practices on tornado sheltering.

guides.title: Tornado Safety: Take Shelter Immediately
guides.summary: Get to the lowest level of a sturdy building, in an interior room away from windows, and protect your head and neck.
guides.whenToUse: Use during a tornado warning or when you observe tornado signs and shelter is available.

guides.preferredOption: Get to a sturdy building before the tornado reaches you if there is time and a safe route.
guides.fallbackOption: If no sturdy shelter is reachable, get low in a safer nearby low area only if conditions make that safer than remaining exposed.
steps: Monitor official alerts and decide early.
Leave mobile homes for sturdy shelter as soon as warnings are issued.
If driving and a sturdy building is nearby, go there promptly.
If no shelter is reachable, protect your head and neck and get as low as practical.
warnings: Vehicles and mobile homes can overturn or be struck by debris.
Low areas may also flood in some storms.
whatNotToDo: Do not try to outrun a close tornado in traffic.
Do not shelter under bridges or overpasses.

sourceReferences.title: What to do During a Tornado
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Location-specific shelter instructions.

sourceReferences.title: Stay Safe During a Tornado
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Public safety actions and scenario guidance.

sourceReferences.title: Joint Statement on Tornado Safety
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Consensus best practices on tornado sheltering.

guides.title: Tornado Safety: If You Are in a Vehicle or Mobile Home
guides.summary: A sturdy building is safer than a vehicle or mobile home. Leave for shelter if you can do so early and safely.
guides.whenToUse: Use during a tornado warning when you are in a car, truck, RV, or mobile home.

guides.preferredOption: Check for injuries, avoid unstable structures, and follow official instructions.
steps: Check yourself and others for injuries.
Avoid entering heavily damaged structures.
Stay away from downed power lines and gas leaks.
Follow official shelter, cleanup, or evacuation instructions.
warnings: Secondary hazards often injure people after the tornado itself has passed.
whatNotToDo: Do not use open flames around possible gas leaks.
Do not re-enter unstable buildings casually.
redFlags: Heavy bleeding, unconsciousness, trouble breathing, gas smell, and structural collapse require emergency help.

sourceReferences.title: What to do During a Tornado
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Location-specific shelter instructions.

sourceReferences.title: Stay Safe During a Tornado
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Public safety actions and scenario guidance.

sourceReferences.title: Joint Statement on Tornado Safety
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Consensus best practices on tornado sheltering.

guides.title: After the Tornado
guides.summary: Watch for structural collapse, gas leaks, broken glass, and downed lines after a tornado passes.
guides.whenToUse: Use once immediate tornado danger has passed.

guides.preferredOption: Evacuate promptly via official routes before roads become blocked.
steps: Monitor local alerts and maps.
Keep a go-bag, medicines, documents, and pet supplies ready.
Leave early if an evacuation order is issued.
Drive with windows up and ventilation on recirculate if smoke is present.
warnings: Roads can become blocked quickly by smoke, congestion, or fire.
Embers can ignite vehicles and structures.
whatNotToDo: Do not wait until the last minute if an order is issued.
Do not assume familiar roads will remain open.
redFlags: Chest pain, severe breathing trouble, worsening chronic symptoms, or burns require urgent help.

sourceReferences.title: Wildfire Evacuation Outreach Materials
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical evacuation planning and public action checklists.

sourceReferences.title: How Wildfire Smoke Affects Your Body
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Health risks and high-risk group guidance.

sourceReferences.title: Public Health Advice During Wildfires
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Public-health smoke and safety guidance.

guides.title: Wildfire Evacuation: Leave Early
guides.summary: If officials order evacuation or fire conditions become unsafe, leave early using planned routes.
guides.whenToUse: Use during active wildfire threats, evacuation warnings or orders, or when smoke and fire make remaining unsafe.

guides.preferredOption: Stay indoors with windows and doors closed and use filtered or cleaner indoor air if possible.
steps: Check air quality and official alerts.
Close windows, doors, and outside vents if conditions allow.
Use HEPA filtration or the cleanest indoor air space available.
Limit physical activity and time outdoors.
Pay extra attention to children, older adults, pregnant people, and anyone with heart or lung disease.
warnings: Wildfire smoke can worsen asthma, COPD, and heart conditions.
whatNotToDo: Do not rely on a damp cloth as true smoke protection.
Do not do strenuous outdoor activity in heavy smoke unless absolutely necessary.
redFlags: Chest pain, severe breathing trouble, worsening chronic symptoms, or burns require urgent help.

sourceReferences.title: Wildfire Evacuation Outreach Materials
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical evacuation planning and public action checklists.

sourceReferences.title: How Wildfire Smoke Affects Your Body
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Health risks and high-risk group guidance.

sourceReferences.title: Public Health Advice During Wildfires
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Public-health smoke and safety guidance.

guides.title: Wildfire Smoke: Protect Your Air
guides.summary: If you are not evacuating, reduce smoke exposure indoors and avoid unnecessary time outside.
guides.whenToUse: Use when wildfire smoke is affecting air quality near your home or shelter.

guides.preferredOption: Wait for official clearance and return with protective gear, water, phone power, and basic supplies.
steps: Confirm re-entry is allowed.
Wear sturdy shoes, gloves, and a respirator if ash or smoke remain heavy.
Check for hot spots, unstable trees, utility damage, and structural hazards.
Use caution around ash, debris, and contaminated water or food.
warnings: Ash and damaged utilities can still harm you after the flames have passed.
whatNotToDo: Do not turn on damaged gas or electrical systems casually.
Do not let children play in ash or debris.
redFlags: Gas smell, active hot spots, unstable structures, or heavy persistent smoke need professional help.

sourceReferences.title: Wildfire Evacuation Outreach Materials
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical evacuation planning and public action checklists.

sourceReferences.title: How Wildfire Smoke Affects Your Body
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Health risks and high-risk group guidance.

sourceReferences.title: Public Health Advice During Wildfires
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Public-health smoke and safety guidance.

guides.title: Returning After Wildfire
guides.summary: Return only when authorities say it is safe, and watch for ash, unstable structures, utility damage, and lingering smoke.
guides.whenToUse: Use after evacuation or smoke sheltering once officials begin allowing re-entry.

guides.preferredOption: Call emergency services immediately and begin rapid cooling with cool water, cold packs, and fanning.
steps: Call emergency services or get urgent help immediately.
Move the person to shade, air conditioning, or the coolest available place.
Apply cool water and cold packs to the neck, armpits, and groin if available.
Fan the person continuously while cooling.
Do not give fluids if the person is vomiting, confused, or not fully alert.
warnings: Heat stroke can rapidly cause organ failure and death.
Delay makes outcomes worse.
whatNotToDo: Do not leave the person alone.
Do not force fluids into someone who is not fully awake.
redFlags: Confusion, seizures, fainting, hot dry skin, severe vomiting, or collapse require emergency care now.

sourceReferences.title: Plan and Respond to a Heat Emergency
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Concise emergency cooling and EMS actions.

sourceReferences.title: Heat Cramps, Heat Exhaustion, Heat Stroke
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Clear symptom tiers and first aid actions.

sourceReferences.title: First Aid for Heat Illness
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Visual signs and immediate public guidance.

guides.title: Heat Stroke: Cool and Call for Help
guides.summary: Heat stroke is an emergency. Call for help, move the person to a cooler place, and cool them aggressively while help is on the way.
guides.whenToUse: Use when someone in hot conditions has confusion, collapse, seizures, or very high body temperature.

guides.preferredOption: Rest in shade or air conditioning, loosen clothing, cool the skin, and sip cool water if the person is alert.
steps: Move to a cool or shaded place.
Loosen or remove extra clothing.
Cool the skin with water and airflow.
Give small sips of cool water if the person is alert and not vomiting.
Watch closely for worsening symptoms.
warnings: Heat exhaustion can progress to heat stroke.
whatNotToDo: Do not ignore persistent vomiting, confusion, or collapse.
Do not send the person back into heat quickly.
redFlags: Symptoms lasting more than a short period, chest pain, fainting, worsening confusion, or severe vomiting require medical evaluation.

sourceReferences.title: Plan and Respond to a Heat Emergency
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Concise emergency cooling and EMS actions.

sourceReferences.title: Heat Cramps, Heat Exhaustion, Heat Stroke
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Clear symptom tiers and first aid actions.

sourceReferences.title: First Aid for Heat Illness
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Visual signs and immediate public guidance.

guides.title: Heat Exhaustion: Cool Down and Rehydrate
guides.summary: Move to a cooler place, rest, and sip fluids if the person is alert. Escalate if symptoms worsen or do not improve.
guides.whenToUse: Use for heavy sweating, weakness, dizziness, cramps, nausea, or fatigue in hot conditions when the person is still alert.

guides.preferredOption: Plan activities for cooler times, drink regularly, rest in shade, and monitor people at higher risk.
steps: Drink regularly and do not wait until you are very thirsty.
Wear lightweight, breathable clothing.
Schedule heavy activity for cooler hours when possible.
Take shade or cooling breaks frequently.
Check children, older adults, and people with chronic illness regularly.
warnings: Heat illness can develop even in people who seem healthy, especially during heat waves.
whatNotToDo: Do not leave people or pets in parked vehicles.

sourceReferences.title: Plan and Respond to a Heat Emergency
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Concise emergency cooling and EMS actions.

sourceReferences.title: Heat Cramps, Heat Exhaustion, Heat Stroke
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Clear symptom tiers and first aid actions.

sourceReferences.title: First Aid for Heat Illness
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Visual signs and immediate public guidance.

guides.title: Heat Safety and Prevention
guides.summary: Hydration, shade, lighter clothing, pacing, and checking vulnerable people reduce the risk of heat illness.
guides.whenToUse: Use before outdoor work, travel, sports, or heat waves.

guides.preferredOption: Get the person into warm, dry shelter, remove wet clothes, insulate with dry layers, and warm the chest, neck, and groin first.
steps: Move the person out of cold, wind, and wet conditions.
Remove wet clothing gently and dry the skin if possible.
Wrap with dry blankets or layers, focusing on the core body first.
Give warm sweetened fluids only if the person is fully awake and can swallow safely.
Get urgent medical help if symptoms are severe or worsening.
warnings: Severe hypothermia can be fatal.
Wet clothing and wind accelerate heat loss.
whatNotToDo: Do not give alcohol.
Do not use direct intense heat on numb skin.
Do not give fluids to an unconscious person.
redFlags: Shivering stops, confusion worsens, slow breathing, loss of consciousness, or inability to rewarm require emergency help.

sourceReferences.title: Extreme Cold Guide
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical recognition and warming steps.

sourceReferences.title: Hypothermia and Frostbite
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Step-by-step emergency response and clear do/don't guidance.

sourceReferences.title: Cold Stress Guide
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Pre-medical arrival handling of cold stress cases.

guides.title: Hypothermia: Warm the Core and Get Help
guides.summary: Move the person to a warm, dry place, remove wet clothing, insulate them, and focus on warming the core while seeking help if symptoms are severe.
guides.whenToUse: Use when cold-exposed people show intense shivering, confusion, slurred speech, clumsiness, or unusual drowsiness.

guides.preferredOption: Protect the area from further cold and seek medical care, especially for extensive or severe frostbite.
steps: Move the person to shelter and protect the affected body part.
Remove wet gloves, socks, or tight items carefully.
Pad and protect the area from pressure and further cold.
Seek medical evaluation for significant frostbite.
warnings: Refreezing after warming can make damage much worse.
Walking on frostbitten feet can increase injury.
whatNotToDo: Do not rub the area.
Do not use direct high heat or hot water casually.
Do not puncture blisters.
redFlags: Hard, waxy skin, large blisters, spreading numbness, or severe pain after rewarming need medical care.

sourceReferences.title: Extreme Cold Guide
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical recognition and warming steps.

sourceReferences.title: Hypothermia and Frostbite
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Step-by-step emergency response and clear do/don't guidance.

sourceReferences.title: Cold Stress Guide
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Pre-medical arrival handling of cold stress cases.

guides.title: Frostbite: Protect and Rewarm Safely
guides.summary: Protect the affected area from further freezing and do not rub it. Rewarm gently when refreezing is unlikely.
guides.whenToUse: Use when skin becomes numb, pale, waxy, hard, or blistered after cold exposure.

guides.preferredOption: Use layered clothing with moisture management and keep backup dry gear available.
steps: Wear layered clothing with moisture-wicking and insulating materials when possible.
Keep spare dry socks, gloves, and a hat available.
Protect from wind and wet conditions early.
Limit time exposed in severe cold or wind chill.
Check companions frequently for early symptoms.
warnings: Cold injuries can develop faster in wind, wet conditions, exhaustion, or poor nutrition.
whatNotToDo: Do not rely on cotton alone in wet, cold conditions if better options are available.

sourceReferences.title: Extreme Cold Guide
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical recognition and warming steps.

sourceReferences.title: Hypothermia and Frostbite
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Step-by-step emergency response and clear do/don't guidance.

sourceReferences.title: Cold Stress Guide
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Pre-medical arrival handling of cold stress cases.

guides.title: Cold Weather Prevention and Layering
guides.summary: Stay dry, layer clothing, protect extremities, and limit exposure during cold, wet, or windy conditions.
guides.whenToUse: Use before winter travel, outdoor work, trekking, storms, or prolonged cold-weather exposure.

guides.preferredOption: Apply direct pressure first, then gently clean and cover the wound.
steps: Wash hands if possible.
Press gently but firmly with a clean cloth or gauze until bleeding slows.
Rinse the wound with clean water.
Apply a clean covering or bandage.
Monitor for signs of infection.
warnings: Dirty wounds can become infected.
whatNotToDo: Do not repeatedly peel off forming clots just to check the wound.
redFlags: Deep wounds, exposed fat or bone, numbness, or signs of infection need medical evaluation.

sourceReferences.title: 2024 AHA/Red Cross First Aid Guidelines
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Latest evidence-based first aid guidance.

sourceReferences.title: Cuts and Puncture Wounds
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Clear government guidance on minor wounds and infection prevention.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency Wound Care PSA
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Concise public guidance on pressure, cleaning, and bandaging.

guides.title: Minor Cuts and Scrapes: Clean and Cover
guides.summary: For minor wounds, stop the bleeding, rinse gently, and cover with a clean dressing.
guides.whenToUse: Use for minor cuts, scrapes, and small punctures with controllable bleeding.

guides.preferredOption: Apply steady firm pressure with the cleanest material available and hold it without checking too often.
steps: Place clean cloth or gauze over the wound.
Press firmly and keep pressure steady.
Keep the person calm and still as practical.
If bleeding slows, continue protecting the wound and seek help if severity is uncertain.
warnings: Heavy bleeding can become life-threatening quickly.
Loss of blood can lead to shock.
whatNotToDo: Do not keep lifting the dressing to check every few seconds.
redFlags: Spurting blood, soaking through multiple dressings, pale or cold skin, confusion, or collapse require urgent help.

sourceReferences.title: 2024 AHA/Red Cross First Aid Guidelines
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Latest evidence-based first aid guidance.

sourceReferences.title: Cuts and Puncture Wounds
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Clear government guidance on minor wounds and infection prevention.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency Wound Care PSA
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Concise public guidance on pressure, cleaning, and bandaging.

guides.title: Bleeding Control: Direct Pressure First
guides.summary: Firm direct pressure is the first step for most external bleeding.
guides.whenToUse: Use when a wound is bleeding more than a simple scrape but can still be directly compressed.

guides.preferredOption: Call emergency services or get urgent help while someone applies firm direct pressure.
steps: Call for emergency help or direct someone to do it.
Apply firm direct pressure immediately.
Keep the person lying down if possible and monitor breathing and responsiveness.
Continue pressure until advanced help takes over or bleeding is clearly controlled.
warnings: Uncontrolled severe bleeding can be fatal in minutes.
whatNotToDo: Do not delay emergency help while searching for perfect supplies.
redFlags: Loss of consciousness, trouble breathing, severe weakness, or ongoing uncontrolled bleeding are immediate emergencies.

guides.title: Severe Bleeding: Get Help Fast
guides.summary: Severe bleeding is an emergency. Apply direct pressure immediately and get emergency help.
guides.whenToUse: Use when blood is spurting, soaking through dressings rapidly, or the person shows signs of shock or collapse.

guides.preferredOption: Seek urgent medical care for deep, jagged, heavily contaminated, numb, or infected wounds.
steps: Assess depth, contamination, bleeding, pain, and function.
Look for numbness, loss of movement, exposed tissue, or severe contamination.
Watch for infection signs such as spreading redness, swelling, pus, or fever.
Get medical help if these signs are present or if you are unsure.
warnings: Delay can worsen infection, tissue damage, and healing.
whatNotToDo: Do not attempt invasive field closure or advanced wound procedures on your own.
redFlags: Exposed bone or fat, persistent bleeding, numbness, severe contamination, spreading infection, or systemic illness require urgent care.

sourceReferences.title: 2024 AHA/Red Cross First Aid Guidelines
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Latest evidence-based first aid guidance.

sourceReferences.title: Cuts and Puncture Wounds
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Clear government guidance on minor wounds and infection prevention.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency Wound Care PSA
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Concise public guidance on pressure, cleaning, and bandaging.

guides.title: When a Wound Needs Urgent Medical Care
guides.summary: Some wounds need urgent professional care even if bleeding is partly controlled.
guides.whenToUse: Use when deciding whether a cut, puncture, or wound can be managed simply or needs urgent care.

guides.preferredOption: Filter out visible particles first if needed, then boil the water vigorously long enough according to trusted emergency guidance.
guides.fallbackOption: If you cannot boil, use an approved backup method such as emergency disinfection guidance with the correct product and dosage.
steps: Pre-filter cloudy water if possible.
Bring water to a vigorous boil.
Maintain boiling according to trusted guidance.
Let it cool and store it in a clean covered container.
warnings: Boiling does not remove chemicals, fuel, or heavy metals.
whatNotToDo: Do not assume boiling fixes water that smells of fuel or chemicals.
Do not store treated water in dirty containers.
redFlags: Fuel smell, chemical odor, unusual discoloration after treatment, or persistent illness after drinking require safer alternatives and official guidance.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency Disinfection of Drinking Water
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Step-by-step emergency boiling/bleach methods.

sourceReferences.title: How to Make Water Safe in an Emergency
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Confirms boiling as primary and explains treatment limits.

sourceReferences.title: How to Create and Store an Emergency Water Supply
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Storage quantities and container guidance.

guides.title: Purify Water by Boiling
guides.summary: Boiling is the most reliable emergency way to make microbiologically unsafe water safer to drink when the main problem is germs rather than chemicals.
guides.whenToUse: Use when official guidance says water may be contaminated by germs or when you suspect biological contamination and have a heat source.

guides.preferredOption: Use official emergency disinfection instructions exactly, including product type, amount, mixing, and wait time.
guides.fallbackOption: If you are unsure of the bleach type or strength, use a different safer source or treatment method rather than guessing.
steps: Pre-filter cloudy water if possible.
Check that the bleach is unscented and appropriate for emergency water disinfection.
Follow official dosage guidance for the bleach strength and water amount.
Mix well and wait the recommended time before use.
warnings: Wrong bleach type or wrong amount can be unsafe.
Bleach treatment does not remove chemicals or fuel contamination.
whatNotToDo: Do not use scented or specialty bleach products.
Do not mix bleach with other cleaners.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency Disinfection of Drinking Water
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Step-by-step emergency boiling/bleach methods.

sourceReferences.title: How to Make Water Safe in an Emergency
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Confirms boiling as primary and explains treatment limits.

sourceReferences.whyUseful: Storage quantities and container guidance.

guides.title: Disinfect Water with Bleach
guides.summary: If you cannot boil water, emergency disinfection with the correct unscented household bleach and dosage may be a backup option.
guides.whenToUse: Use only when boiling is not possible and you have the right unscented bleach and official dosage guidance for the strength.

guides.preferredOption: Store a multi-day supply of clean water in safe containers and rotate it regularly.
steps: Use food-grade containers or trusted sealed water supplies.
Label and date stored water.
Keep water in a cool, protected location.
Rotate or refresh stored supplies according to trusted guidance.
preparednessTips: Plan extra water for hot climates, children, medical needs, and pets.

guides.title: Emergency Water Storage
guides.summary: Store emergency water before disasters so you are not forced to improvise under pressure.
guides.whenToUse: Use for home, vehicle, workplace, or travel preparedness planning.

guides.preferredOption: Build a ready-to-grab kit with enough core supplies for at least several days.
steps: Store water and non-perishable food.
Add lighting, batteries, radio, first aid basics, medicines, hygiene items, and a whistle.
Keep copies of important documents and emergency contacts.
Include local maps, phone charging options, and basic tools.
Check the kit regularly and replace expired items.

guides.title: Build a 72-Hour Emergency Kit
guides.summary: Build a simple grab-and-stay emergency kit with water, food, light, first aid basics, medicines, documents, and communication tools.
guides.whenToUse: Use before disasters such as earthquakes, floods, wildfire, storms, or outages.

guides.preferredOption: Choose meeting points, assign roles, and pick an out-of-area contact.
steps: Discuss likely local hazards and what each person should do.
Choose household and neighborhood meeting points.
Pick an out-of-area contact who can relay information.
Write down key phone numbers and addresses.
Practice the plan periodically.

guides.title: Make a Family Emergency Plan
guides.summary: A good emergency plan tells everyone how to reconnect, where to meet, and what to do if communication fails.
guides.whenToUse: Use for household preparedness before disasters or trips.

guides.preferredOption: Pack a portable bag with essentials you can carry quickly.
steps: Pack medicines, documents, chargers, water, snacks, basic hygiene items, flashlight, and cash.
Include clothing layers and protective footwear if space allows.
Add child, pet, or disability-specific items if needed.
Store the bag near an exit and review it regularly.

sourceReferences.whyUseful: Family-focused planning, kits, and drills.

guides.title: Evacuation Go-Bag Checklist
guides.summary: Keep a fast-grab bag ready for evacuations so you are not scrambling during a crisis.
guides.whenToUse: Use where sudden evacuation is possible due to fire, flood, earthquake damage, storms, or conflict.

guides.preferredOption: Move everyone into the safest indoor area available and maintain communication, supplies, and situational awareness.
steps: Bring people, pets, and essential supplies inside.
Close doors, windows, and vents if the hazard involves outdoor contamination or smoke.
Use official updates to know when conditions change.
Conserve water, power, and phone battery if disruption may last.

sourceReferences.title: How to Prepare for Emergencies
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Family-focused planning, kits, and drills.

guides.title: Shelter in Place Basics
guides.summary: Sometimes staying inside is safer than evacuating. Prepare to remain indoors with supplies and information.
guides.whenToUse: Use for storms, hazardous air, unrest, utility disruptions, or other cases where official guidance says staying inside is safer.

guides.preferredOption: Stay in the safest practical area and build simple visible and audible signals rescuers can notice.
guides.fallbackOption: If you have a mirror, flashlight, or whistle, use short repeated signaling bursts while conserving energy and battery.
steps: Stay calm, assess injuries, and decide whether staying put improves your chance of rescue.
Create visible contrast with clothing, rocks, logs, or lights if possible.
Use whistle blasts or repeated light signals to draw attention.
Balance signaling with shelter, hydration, and warmth needs.
warnings: Wandering can make you harder to find and increase injury or exposure risk.
whatNotToDo: Do not keep moving without a clear survival reason.
Do not waste all battery power early.
preparednessTips: Carry a whistle, mirror, light, and tell someone your route before travel.

sourceReferences.title: General Guidance on Emergency Communication Strategies
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Useful communication concepts, but not ideal as a primary wilderness signaling source.

sourceReferences.title: Recommended Emergency Preparedness Guidelines for Rail Transit Systems
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Contains signaling and coordination patterns, but source fit for lost-person rescue needs review.

guides.title: Signal for Rescue: Stay Visible and Be Heard
guides.summary: If you are lost or stranded, make yourself easier to locate with visible, audible, and light-based signals while protecting yourself from the environment.
guides.whenToUse: Use when you are separated from your group, stranded, injured, or unable to self-rescue safely.

guides.preferredOption: If staying put improves safety, shelter, and signaling, remain near your known location.
guides.fallbackOption: Move only if staying is clearly more dangerous or if movement meaningfully improves shelter, water, or rescue chances.
steps: Assess injury, weather, terrain, water, daylight, and communication options.
Choose the safer option rather than the option that feels more active.
If staying, improve shelter and signaling.
If moving, do so deliberately and leave clear signs of your route if possible.
warnings: Panic movement can increase exposure and separation from rescuers.
whatNotToDo: Do not wander aimlessly.
Do not abandon a strong shelter or signal position without a reason.

sourceReferences.title: General Guidance on Emergency Communication Strategies
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Useful communication concepts, but not ideal as a primary wilderness signaling source.

sourceReferences.title: Recommended Emergency Preparedness Guidelines for Rail Transit Systems
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Contains signaling and coordination patterns, but source fit for lost-person rescue needs review.

guides.title: Stay Put vs Move
guides.summary: In many lost-person situations, staying near the last known point can improve rescue chances, but there are exceptions.
guides.whenToUse: Use when deciding whether to stay in place or move after becoming lost or stranded.

guides.preferredOption: Use short repeated light signals while preserving battery for longer-term survival needs.
steps: Use repeated short flashes rather than leaving a light on continuously if battery is limited.
Balance signaling with warmth, shelter, and hydration needs.
Keep a reserve of battery for later if rescue may be delayed.
whatNotToDo: Do not drain your only light source immediately if rescue may take time.

sourceReferences.title: General Guidance on Emergency Communication Strategies
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Useful communication concepts, but not ideal as a primary wilderness signaling source.

sourceReferences.title: Recommended Emergency Preparedness Guidelines for Rail Transit Systems
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Contains signaling and coordination patterns, but source fit for lost-person rescue needs review.

guides.title: Night Signaling and Battery Conservation
guides.summary: At night, light signals can help rescuers find you, but battery and warmth conservation still matter.
guides.whenToUse: Use when stranded after dark or when aerial or distant visual spotting is possible.

guides.title: Power Outage Basics
guides.summary: Basic lighting, food, refrigeration, charging, and safety actions during blackouts.
guides.whenToUse: Use during unplanned loss of electricity at home or work.

guides.title: Communication Outage Basics
guides.summary: Fallback communication habits when networks are overloaded or unavailable.
guides.whenToUse: Use when cell, internet, or local communication networks fail during emergencies.

guides.title: Sanitation and Waste Handling Basics
guides.summary: Clean water, hand hygiene, and waste handling basics during disruption.
guides.whenToUse: Use when toilets, water service, or sewer service are disrupted.

guides.title: Food Safety Without Refrigeration
guides.summary: Food handling priorities when refrigeration and cooking are limited.
guides.whenToUse: Use during outages, evacuations, and prolonged sheltering.

replitNotes: Import this pack as the master local knowledge seed file.
Do not delete the original reference guides when merging.
Use layer + cardType + category + riskLevel + sourceQuality in retrieval and filtering.
For practical/emergency queries, rank action cards first.
For medical_safety queries, use conservative templates and keep warnings/red flags prominent.
Hide or demote guides with contentStatus = needs_source_review from production answers until sources are improved.
It is okay to browse plannedExpansion topics in the library, but they should not be surfaced as strong matches until fully written.
